speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three with Rose Kohn. So two weeks you were there. And then how did you leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they selected us again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a selection. We all came out naked in the-- in the-- on that platz, what do you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: The place in front.  I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The place out-- outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a German woman, you know, a rotten-- [LAUGHS] you know, and she was the selector.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Nazi.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Nazi. Yeah. So she was selecting. Of course, I was young. And I wasn't that-- that skinny. I was never skinny, you know, so I went to the right. Mother stands behind me. And I hear how she stops her. You old thing, away with you. Those were the words. I still remember hearing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. And at this point, this second, split of a second, somebody tapped her. A Nazi came and tapped her on the back with-- with-- with some papers and she turned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother stood there. She didn't go, because she didn't understand-- I don't know what happened. There was one guy, a Nazi, sitting up there, standing. He came over to her. And he said to her, can you work? Are you strong? She said, of course, of course, and sent her over.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a miracle. Two-- my mother had two miracles, two miracles. And-- and she made it with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old would your mother have been at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she was about 53. So people her age did not make it. They did not make it really. And what he did-- I-- I said she must have reminded him of an aunt, of a mother, of somebody to do something like that. I-- I just could never-- I'll never forget it. Anyway, so we were selected to go to Bergen-Belsen. They took a bunch of us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by train. By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make you walk--

speaker: interviewee
text: And gave us a little better clothes, kind of, better than-- than there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had described before-- let me just get a detail down here. You had described before that they gave you the top of a pajama or the bottom. Are you referring to the uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clothes. OK. It was just other people's clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Whatever they threw. They threw at you. They threw at you, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then when you were going then to Bergen-Belsen, did they give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave--

speaker: interviewee
text: The uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was  a uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was a uniform, yes. That's what I-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never did-- they never did brand you. They never did give you a number.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they couldn't. Too many people were coming in. This was already almost the end of Auschwitz too, you know, the end of the year. They discontinued it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're talking about either late July, early August of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. That's right. That's right. It was summer time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you walk to the train, do you remember? Or were the trains right there to go to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, I think, not-- not too much. The bad walking was when we arrived to Bergen-Belsen. This is what I-- and I didn't eat. I absolutely could not. The smell, it could be the smell of these burned bodies or fear, you know. I stopped eating. I couldn't. Just water. I begged for water, but I couldn't get water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived to Bergen-Belsen, and they told us to get out and the Nazis came with those big, big dogs, with the wolfs, and they asked us, who cannot walk, is sick, should tell him so they're gonna put us on a-- on a bus. I was afraid to say it. I couldn't walk. But I didn't say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I would say, it would be good, because this was not a bad place. This was not, at that time, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, it wasn't a trick? They really would have taken you by bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you were right, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. But I was so afraid. So my mother and my friends were holding me and helping me.  We had to walk a few miles, quite a few miles to it. And we arrived there, and we lived in tents, tents and straw. We slept on straw. And-- and we weren't treated badly. No. It wasn't-- it wasn't too bad over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were given nice meals, nice. What happened? They wanted us strong to send out to work, so they didn't want weak and sick people. Were you feeling-- could you eat there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then-- then. Yeah. Yeah. My appetite came back, and I could eat, yeah. Mhm. And I got back to myself. And-- and then-- and then we stayed there till they started to build barracks for all the other Auschwitz people, when-- when-- when Bergen-Belsen became hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we left, you know, I mean, every few weeks another group-- how they remembered, because their-- their organization is so great, the Germans. They knew exactly who came to be sent out, because so many people arrived there, you know? And I-- we were the last group to go, me and my mother and a few other people. Out of Auschwitz into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of Bergen-Belsen. Out of Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How long were you actually in Bergen-Belsen before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Let's see. August, September, October-- October, or half of November or the end of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing. Nothing. We just did nothing. As I said, it-- it was-- but we were told then that they want us to be strong because they need us for work. But by the time people were coming in from Auschwitz, other people, but this was not the selected group anymore. The other people stayed over there till they all died and Bergen-Belsen was a horrible place, horrible. You know, so many people died out and from sicknesses and diseases. And-- but we were the lucky ones to get out, me and my mother and a few others.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take your from there, from Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a-- on a-- on a train. On the train. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were going and going. And-- and-- and there were air attacks, air attacks. They were bombing the trains. And we thought that's the end too. I mean, it really looked like the end, because everything was shaking, you know, and all the smoke and everything. Some people probably got killed, you know. But-- and it was cold. But we arrived to Germany. Bergen-Belsen was Germany too. We arrived to Geislingen. It was a place called Geislingen. It's a small city, and they had an ammunition factory there. And so we were taken to work over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you-- where were you barracked? Where were you housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a special camp, also specially made camp. We are housed there. And it was clean. We had to clean it, to get up early in the morning to make our beds and keep clean. And-- but the work was horrible, horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to sit night-- I worked nights, for 12 hours. I had to sit at that machine, a boring machine, and make holes in the guns, in the guns so-- and-- what do you call, nail-- a nail could come in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was my job. And a Nazi woman sat and watched us all night, no food, cold. I mean, the place was big because they were driving there with-- with trucks. And no sleep and no food. And my-- sleep to me was everything. If I couldn't sleep, then I couldn't function.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. I-- I felt there that I am not going to make it. I really felt these nights and the walking home and standing on appels, you know, 5:00 in the morning, you know, for hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two hours. Two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the afternoon--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told us get up and make the beds and get up out of an appel. There was no sleep, not much sleep at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was with me. Mhm. Mhm. And I had frozen toes. They froze. And the pain was so terrible, you know. Oh, it-- it was bad, anyway. But this was already towards the end of the war, because I was there from, let's see, November till April.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Geislingen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Geislingen, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- just one little detail. When you would walk back and forth, did you have shoes? What kind of shoes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had those Holland, Holland shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wooden shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dutch. Yeah. The wooden-- wooden shoes. Yeah. But just that-- we had only those-- those prisoners' gowns-- you know, clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No coats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was cold. And oh, god. Yeah. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother managed to survive this?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a strong woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I bet.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a strong woman, very strong. She survived because she-- she-- she buried sisters with the grippe that it was at, you know, at time that everybody died, and-- and a mother and a brother. And I mean, she was a very strong woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a question that's on a more personal level. What was your relationship with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good. I mean, she-- to her, I was the apple of her eye. But she was a domineering woman, very domineering. My-- my kids remember that. Yeah. And she was with me all the time, because I couldn't-- I couldn't do any other way. She was just too devoted, too good. And I loved her, but she was hard to live with, hard to live with.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were together--

speaker: interviewee
text: But a good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. She was a good person. And so when you were together in the barracks, in the work-- working, and the marches and everything, you would just huddle together and hold each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We worked different shifts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you did work different shifts? She worked in the daytime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She worked in the daytime, yeah. But she came all the time to tell me how we're gonna die. We're gonna die. We're not going to make it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you say to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, stop it. I said, stop it. Don't talk like that. Stop it. I mean, each time, we're not going to make-- very, very pessimistic, fear, fear. She had a rough life. Mother had a rough life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become kind of the mother in this situation, because she was so fearful and you had to keep your spirits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I was the strong-- I was stronger, yeah. I was stronger then. And I had hope. I-- I didn't give up hope. I really felt-- and then I said to myself, look. I-- I was always afraid of dying as a kid. Always fear, fear of dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- as a child you had that?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, fear of dying always, always fear, even older. And at that time, there was no fear of dying, absolutely not. Everything left me. And I said to myself, well, look, you're gonna learn the mystery of life, the mystery. This is how I went with, with positive, like positive. Positive attitude. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work nights the whole time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There. Mhm. Mhm. 12 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put the younger people at night? Is that how they did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It didn't matter. It just didn't matter. They just made shifts, different shifts. And so Mother had it a little better, you know, than I, because she could, you know, sleep a little. Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it went like that from mid-November through April. Any-- at the point at which in April-- did-- had you started to hear rumors about the end of the war? You had seen bombings and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- I don't think so. I don't think we did. You know, we were there-- I didn't hear anything. But I know they said they're taking us to Tyrol. Tyrol is-- is in the mountains in Germany or in Austria. And that's where they're gonna take us. And so finally, they did on the train. And we went through Allach. I don't know you're familiar with Allach, not far from Dachau was also a prison, Allach. And we stayed one overnight there. And then I saw an-- an air attack from far. God, it was a big one, a big one. And-- and they put us on the trains. And we were on these trains, going back and forth. We don't know-- we-- we knew Tyrol, but where and what, we had no idea. We thought they're gonna dispose of us then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this group, in this transport that you're describing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. A few hundred. A few hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mostly women, men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Women. Women.

speaker: interviewer
text: All women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- only women. Yeah. I saw men while in the trains, when the train stopped and there was another train. I saw how they were disposing of bodies, so many men, throwing out, you know, dead, dead bodies from the trains. I saw them coming out. But in our trains, all of sudden, you know-- you know, who were our guides were the-- the-- the Hungarian Nazis. There were no more Germans watching us. The Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they explain why the change?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody talked on it. They didn't talk. They wouldn't say a word. And there was a big raid also when we're in the trains, and-- and the Americans, I mean, by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Air raid, you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans came at that time. But a day before, a few days before, all of a sudden they're announcing that the Red Cross is coming to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is still in Geislingen? Or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's already on the trains, going to Tyrol.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on the train. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train. We're going to Tyrol.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, probably, were-- I don't know, a week, two, maybe two weeks, something like that, back and forth. And they came, the Red Cross. And each one of us got a package, you know, the first time I got something there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was milk-- powdered milk. It was cheese, crackers. What else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Chocolate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember chocolate, no. You know, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Just food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just food, you know. And I remember we went down and made a little fire to make milk, to warm up with-- with water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you make the fire, on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. On the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stopped. They stopped. And we could come out at that time. Yeah. And so there was a little hope. I mean, they-- we already knew something is happening. And then we went back on the trains, and then was the air raid. The Americans came, you know, and they had to-- the-- there Germans were still fighting, shooting back, you know. There was still a fight to catch the train. And some people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people, the prisoners on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoners, yeah. Yeah, in the train. And all of a sudden, [LAUGHS] there are the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing that first American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mhm. I-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not in a plane, obviously. He was, what, in a jeep?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. All-- No, no. They came, yeah. They came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. And they took out the Nazis. And I remember all these-- the women, the women especially-- I paid attention to the women because they were such no-goodniks-- and with their hands up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- oh the SS who had kept you prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the hands up. And they took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you describe to us that first prisoner-- I mean, that first GI that you saw, that first American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was just like God came, you know? It was something unbelievable, something unbelievable that this happened. And-- but they took them away and I was angry. You know, they want-- we wanted more than that, to see-- to see a punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they took-- oh, I see, they took these women away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took all these women away. And we wanted to see more, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You wanted to see them executed or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, something. I mean, something that somebody could take, you know, some revenge. But that's-- well, they were too kind. The Americans were too kind, too nice. And then we were taken on-- on buses to the next village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took you off, the Americans took you off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Americans took us off to a village. And people got into the houses where the German lived. And all of a sudden, they said-- they put out signs that they have already so many people that they couldn't have anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me and my mother and a few others had-- still had to sleep in the-- in the train, which was-- I felt-- I felt it so unfair, you know? I was-- I was not an aggressive, neither was mother, to just go in and say, hey, you just let me stay in a warm-- in a warm room, you know? But we didn't do that, and they didn't do it. The Americans didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans didn't, no. They said that, well, it's already occupied, already occupied. So that made me a little angry. And then they-- they fed us, you know, with the bread, the-- the white bread, what we weren't accustomed to. And we waited a couple days, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still staying on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Yeah. I was always on the train. And I was never-- I was never in a-- but they said the next day they're gonna take us to a camp, to a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at this moment that you saw the American, and-- were you clear that this is it, the war's-- for you, the war is over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Was over. But that was a very sad feeling in my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where am I going? Where are we going? And then the recollection of the family, the recollection of the family, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: All that you'd lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: All that-- yeah. I was very, very kind of-- with-- with the happiness, there was sadness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it all of a sudden just caught up with you, the reality, or the realization of all you'd been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Where are we now? Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: For once you had a minute to catch your breath and see what's you'd lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And then, you know, life went on. We came to the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing, where I-- we met General Eisenhower there. You know. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --this mythic figure, what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my God. Just what an honor, what a beautiful thing to see a general that fought the war and made us free. It was just a feeling of undescribable feeling, really. I was so proud, you know, to see him. It was just something, something to see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was so happy that I got a picture, a guard, I don't know what the photographer, who he was. But he said, I got pictures, do you want to buy some? You know. So I got it. Mhm. Now, when he said, do you want to buy some, how did you have money to buy anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what we had. We had something, something was given to us. You know. Food and-- and clothes was given to us. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an arrangement was there at Feldafing? What-- were you-- were you again in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barracks. We were-- these used to be for the German soldiers, what you call, the barracks for the German soldiers. So we were there. And we were fed a meal every day, cooked meal. And I still remember that meal. It was rice with meat, what I would serve to 10 people today, what I would eat myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So often you hear of people who got sick from getting this food afterward.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got sick. This was-- this was later, a little later what I'm talking. But at the beginning, they got so sick. I saw people from the package only what we got from the Red Cross, people would eat up everything and got very, gravely ill and some died. But me and my mother, we knew how to divide, because I-- that's why we survived, because we took one piece of bread and divide it. You're not eating more than this one piece, so you have tomorrow another little piece. And the same thing I did with the package. So it was easy. We didn't get sick. We never got sick. Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in the DP camp, what did you do? What was the-- was there an order of business? Did you have some job that-- I don't mean job like work, but did you have some goal there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. There was nothing there. I just traveled, you know. I went to Munich. It was not too far from Munich. Some people went there to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: To visit my friends, because they stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us. You know, I mean, they didn't want to stay in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they left Feldafing and went on to Munich. I see.

words.text: and went on to Munich. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to stay in Feldafing. Yeah. Mhm. So I went to visit them. And we-- we talked. We had so many people to talk to and exchange our experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- and then-- and then some people came and told us to get to-- to-- to Switzerland. They said, Switzerland will let you in because you are refugee. So my ex-husband, I was there with him and my mother. And he said, oh, yeah. We all going there, you know? So we went first to visit. And it was a beautiful, beautiful city. You know. What city was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konstanz and Bodensee on the Kreuzlingen border, Kreuzlingen border, about an hour away from Zurich. But who would let us in? Nobody let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you said your ex-husband, so now we have to backtrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet the man who would be your ex-husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: The minute I came to Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on the truck. On the truck to go down, he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had been liberated already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was-- yeah. He was liberated too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had-- pardon me. Where had he been from? What camp had he been in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was in Buchenwald and Auschwitz and Poznan and he was in many camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was already--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Liberated. Was he working in the DP camp, helping people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or he just spotted you on the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Spotted me on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he helped you off the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We came up. But he came into the bunk, to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, yeah, at that time. You know, and you know, it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: He introduced himself. Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was also from Lodz. Had you known him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. My mother knew his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was very stoked that he's also from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she-- so your mother approved of him, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.  In a way. Well, [LAUGHS] it's a lot to talk about. But I'm not saying-- very, very difficult life. Very difficult life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946, in Konstanz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in-- OK. But you didn't stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went to-- we went to-- we moved into this Konstanz and Bodensee to-- you know, because it was a nice little city. And we were helped by the UNRRA. They were helping us. And then we worked a little. You know, he worked a little. He was selling, you know, different things. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things was he selling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he went to those little islands and bought eggs and butter and came back and say, well, always a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he get the money to buy the eggs and butter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't-- we-- just-- at the beginning, you didn't need much. We got something from the UNRRA. So you spend, and you make some profit, and that's how people were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother was with me. Mhm. Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you got pregnant when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pregnant in-- well, let's see, in '47. My son was born in-- let's see, November, November in '47. Yeah. So I had a baby right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son, which son?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leo, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- then were you just-- where did you live in this-- in this place, in-- in Konstanz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Konstanz, we lived-- a German woman rented a room for us, one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again one room, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it's your husband and your baby and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just took care of the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't work. Yeah. Because our needs were so minimal, really. I mean, look-- and food we got from the UNRRA. So-- and he was making a few dollars selling things and buying, yeah. So it wasn't too bad then, after-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide you wanted to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not what I decided. The-- the-- the Joint Distribution Committee came over and then said that they can take us. You know, if we like to go, they will send us to the United States. And we did. We did decide yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they asked me what city? I said, it doesn't matter, I said. I want to be in the United States. So I wound up in St. Louis, Missouri, which I didn't like. I couldn't stand the summers there, you know. So my mother worked. And-- and my ex-husband worked. He was still your husband at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Oh, yeah. And my-- my mother worked. And so we bought some ticket, trains-- tickets and came over to the-- to Los Angeles on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go from-- I mean, I know how you probably went by boat. Did you go by boat from Europe to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hamburg. From Hamburg, we came over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you made any friends of any kind while you were there, in Germany still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We still-- we still in touch. And we-- we're very close. Some are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to leave them to go to a place you knew nothing of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I knew they're coming too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that-- and a lot of them came already. You know, the people from that city, what we lived, yeah, we were about 100 of us. And so a lot I met here, and they're still here. Some are here. Some passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- do you remember your first sight of America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I came in to New Orleans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And what was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have-- like you and your husband and baby had one cabin and your mother was in another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother was sent to Detroit, you see. Because she had a different name, they didn't understand that, you know? So she was sent to Detroit. But they helped me to bring her back, you know, to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she-- did she travel on the same ship with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she went-- that must have been very upsetting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very rough, yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ship crossing OK? Was it comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so sick. Oh. It was terrible. I-- I couldn't-- I was so nauseous, day and night and day and night, never left me. Very, very ill.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your little boy? How was Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was all right, but he stopped eating. He wasn't eating any more. And when we arrived, he got the measles. And after the measles, he got pneumonia. And he had to be taken away from me to the hospital. Yeah. It was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I talk a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what--
